name: Kubernetes Cluster Overview (Conduit default)
description: Default cluster telemetry collected by Conduit's k8s profile: per-node hostmetrics, kubeletstats (node / pod / container CPU + memory + network + filesystem + uptime + limit-relative utilization), filelog/k8s of /var/log/pods/*, and k8sattributes enrichment on every pipeline. Panels key off k8s.pod.name / k8s.namespace.name / k8s.node.name — the platform-native primary keys for k8s telemetry — not host.name. Use the Namespace and Pod preset filters at the top to scope panels; flipping Namespace narrows every panel below at once.

panels.content:
# Kubernetes Cluster Overview

Default cluster telemetry from **Conduit's k8s profile** ([`internal/profiles/k8s/`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/tree/main/internal/profiles/k8s)). Every panel is keyed off the platform-native primary keys for k8s — `k8s.pod.name`, `k8s.namespace.name`, `k8s.node.name`, `k8s.deployment.name` — never `host.name`.

| Section | Source | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster shape | `metrics` | Active pod count by namespace; container uptime (drops to ~0 surface recent restarts) |
| Compute pressure | `metrics` | Top noisy pods by CPU / memory cores; pods hottest as % of their limits; per-namespace rollups |
| Network | `metrics` | Pod network throughput by direction; pod network errors by namespace |
| Filesystem | `metrics` | Top containers by ephemeral-storage usage |
| Logs | `k8s-cluster` | Volume by severity, by namespace, top noisy pods, recent ERROR / WARN stream |

**Filter** by `Namespace` to scope to one team or workload. **Filter** by `Pod` for incident drill-down. **Filter** by `Service` if you've named services consistently across namespaces.

**Two CPU / memory views, on purpose.** The first row in the Compute Pressure section shows raw **cores / bytes** — works for every pod, even those without limits set. The second row shows **% of limit** (`k8s.pod.cpu_limit_utilization`, `k8s.pod.memory_limit_utilization`) — only emits for pods with limits, and answers the right question for an SRE: "is this pod hot relative to what it asked for?". Pods without limits show up in row 1 but not row 2, which is itself useful signal during a capacity review.

**Known gap — container restart counts.** kubeletstats emits `container.uptime` and `k8s.pod.uptime` (Conduit enables both in [`internal/profiles/k8s/kubelet.yaml`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/profiles/k8s/kubelet.yaml)) but not `k8s.container.restarts` — that's a `k8sclusterreceiver` metric. Until Conduit ships k8scluster, the **Container Uptime by Pod** panel below is the restart proxy: a drop to ~0 means a container restarted.

For the cross-platform contract this board satisfies, see [`internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md`](https://github.com/conduit-obs/conduit-agent/blob/main/internal/profiles/PROFILE_SPEC.md).

_Dashboard generated by Conduit V0 MVP. Stored as a checked-in definition under `dashboards/` in the Conduit repo._

panels.name: Active Pod Count by Namespace
panels.description: COUNT_DISTINCT of k8s.pod.name broken down by k8s.namespace.name in the last 5 minutes — the cluster's running shape, with the headline number Datadog and Grafana operators reach for first when they open a board: 'is anything missing?'.

panels.name: Container Uptime by Pod (restart proxy)
panels.description: AVG of container.uptime broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name + k8s.container.name. Read this as 'how long has each container been running?' — drops to ~0 mark restarts. Conduit enables this opt-in metric in internal/profiles/k8s/kubelet.yaml; until we also ship k8sclusterreceiver, this is the closest thing to a restart counter.

panels.name: Top 10 Pods by CPU Cores
panels.description: AVG of k8s.pod.cpu.usage (cores; not a 0..1 fraction — kubeletstats emits CPU in fractional cores) broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name, ordered descending, top 10. The 'what's hot in absolute terms?' panel — works for every pod whether or not it has limits set.

panels.name: Top 10 Pods by Memory Working Set
panels.description: AVG of k8s.pod.memory.working_set (bytes) broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name, ordered descending, top 10. Working set is what the kubelet uses for OOM decisions — the right memory pressure signal for k8s, not raw RSS or usage.

panels.content: ## Compute pressure — % of limit (limit-bearing pods only)

panels.name: Top 10 Pods by CPU Limit Utilization
panels.description: AVG of k8s.pod.cpu_limit_utilization (0..1 fraction; >1 means the pod is being throttled by the kubelet) broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name, top 10. Only emitted for pods that have CPU limits set — pods without limits don't appear here at all, which itself is useful capacity-review signal.

panels.name: Top 10 Pods by Memory Limit Utilization
panels.description: AVG of k8s.pod.memory_limit_utilization (0..1 fraction; reaching 1.0 = OOM kill imminent) broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name, top 10. Same caveat as the CPU panel: only pods with memory limits set show up here, but those are exactly the pods you have actionable signal for.

panels.name: CPU Cores by Namespace
panels.description: SUM of k8s.pod.cpu.usage broken down by k8s.namespace.name — capacity-planning view of which namespaces (teams, environments) consume CPU. Stacked so the cluster-wide total is the top of the chart.

panels.name: Memory Working Set by Namespace
panels.description: SUM of k8s.pod.memory.working_set broken down by k8s.namespace.name. Same shape as the CPU panel above; together the two are the 'who's using the cluster?' read for capacity reviews.

panels.name: Node CPU + Memory
panels.description: AVG of k8s.node.cpu.usage (fractional cores) and k8s.node.memory.working_set (bytes) broken down by k8s.node.name. Node-level pressure that pod-level panels miss — when a node runs hot the scheduler stops fitting new pods there and the cluster autoscaler kicks in (or doesn't).

panels.name: Pod Network I/O by Direction (top 25)
panels.description: SUM of k8s.pod.network.io broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name + direction, ordered descending, top 25. Honeycomb auto-applies INCREASE to the cumulative counter so values are bytes-per-bucket per pod per direction. Top 25 surfaces the chatty pods without overwhelming the legend.

panels.name: Pod Network Errors by Namespace
panels.description: SUM of k8s.pod.network.errors broken down by k8s.namespace.name + direction. Sustained non-zero values usually mean a CNI / overlay issue, not application code — the per-namespace breakdown tells you whether the problem is global (CNI) or scoped to one workload (NetworkPolicy + saturated pod).

panels.name: Top 25 Containers by Ephemeral Storage Usage
panels.description: MAX of container.filesystem.usage broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name + k8s.container.name. Surfaces containers writing heavily to the ephemeral container filesystem — common cause of pod evictions when nodes hit the eviction threshold for nodefs.

panels.name: Log Volume by Severity
panels.description: COUNT of k8s-cluster events broken down by severity_text. Container logs flow through filelog/k8s with the upstream `container` operator and pick up k8sattributes enrichment; severity_text comes from Conduit's transform/logs processor (defaults to INFO when the container itself doesn't tag a level). ERROR + WARN spikes here are the cluster-wide health pulse.

panels.name: Log Volume by Namespace
panels.description: COUNT of k8s-cluster events broken down by k8s.namespace.name. The companion to severity above — when one workload spikes, it shows up here with namespace context, making 'who's flooding?' a single click instead of a query rewrite.

panels.name: Top 25 Pods by Log Volume
panels.description: COUNT of k8s-cluster events broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name + k8s.container.name, ordered descending, top 25 — the noisy-neighbor table operators reach for when the severity panel above just turned red.

panels.name: Recent ERROR + WARN Pods (top 25)
panels.description: COUNT of k8s-cluster events filtered to severity_text in (ERROR, WARN), broken down by k8s.namespace.name + k8s.pod.name + k8s.container.name, top 25. Read as: 'which pods are loudly failing right now'. Click any row in Honeycomb to drop into the underlying events for that pod.
